title: A Berry-Esseen Bound for Quantum Lattice Systems
authors: Marcus Cramer, Fernando G. S. L. Brandão, Mădălin Guţă, Álvaro M. Alhambra, Matteo Scandi
abstract: It is expected that the statistical fluctuations of local observables in large quantum systems obey the central limit theorem, and approximate a normal distribution as their size grows. Here, we prove a version of the Berry-Esseen theorem for quantum lattice systems, which strengthens that central limit theorem by providing a rigorous convergence estimate towards the normal distribution for large but finite system size. Given a local quantum Hamiltonian on $N$ particles and a quantum state with a finite correlation length, the result states that the measurement of local observables such as the energy follows a normal distribution, up to an error scaling as $\mathcal{O}\left(N^{-\frac{1}{2}} \text{polylog}(N)\right)$, which is optimal up to logarithmic factors.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous Berry-Esseen bound for quantum lattice systems, quantifying how quickly the distribution of local observables approaches a Gaussian. It shows that for systems with decaying correlations, the convergence error scales nearly as N^{-1/2}, providing an optimal rate for finite-size quantum systems.
main_problem: Establishing a rigorous Berry-Esseen bound for quantum lattice systems to quantify the rate of convergence of local observable statistics toward a normal distribution.
main_result: The authors prove that for large but finite quantum lattice systems, the distribution of local observables (like energy) approaches a Gaussian with an error scaling as O(N^{-1/2} polylog(N)), which is optimal up to logarithmic factors.
method: The proof utilizes Esseen's inequality, characteristic functions, and a differential equation approach, combined with a peeling procedure and truncated Taylor expansions to manage non-commuting local operators.
model_or_system: Quantum lattice systems with N particles/sites and a local (R-local) Hamiltonian consisting of bounded operators, where the quantum state exhibits decaying spatial correlations (exponential or polynomial).
key_observables: Local observables such as energy or magnetization.
important_parameters: System size N, lattice dimension D, correlation decay rate (alpha), locality radius R, and the variance of the Hamiltonian.
main_assumptions: The quantum state must have sufficiently fast decaying correlations, the Hamiltonian must be local and bounded, and the variance must scale linearly with the system size.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the partitioning of the Hamiltonian into concentric layers based on distance from an anchor point.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the setting of local Hamiltonians and decaying correlations, defines the Berry-Esseen figure of merit, uses Esseen's inequality to transform the problem into a characteristic function analysis, and employs a differential equation and operator decomposition strategy to bound the error terms.
why_interesting: This provides a rigorous foundation for the Central Limit Theorem in many-body dynamics, offering precise error bounds that are crucial for understanding the validity of Gaussian approximations in large-scale quantum simulations and thermodynamic limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Representation of the operators defined in Eq. (62). Each b Hℓ j(m) corresponds to the terms in the Hamiltonian that are in the m-th layer (of width 2Rℓ) starting from j. On the other hand, zℓ j(m) corresponds to the remaining Hamiltonian when m layers have been discarded.
